topic: Two-pole geomagnetic model — toroidal architecture with B(r) equation, flux conservation failure
our_argument_summary: WIN-053 claims the dome's 'Closed Toroidal Ovoid' geometry produces a dipole-like geomagnetic field via a sub-terrestrial aetheric return path, with B(r) = 62,376×e^(−r_N/8619) + 64,852×e^(−r_S/8619) nT. Our review gives the verdict 'Refuted by Data,' arguing: (1) the two-pole model contradicts V50.6's monopolar vortex architecture, (2) the dome provides no unified equations like IGRF or CHAOS-7, and (3) flux conservation forbids equal-strength poles in a ring magnet with a 1,600:1 area ratio. We also describe the toroidal flow circuit in detail_extra.

relevant_files: monitor/baseline/05-model.txt (V51.0 architecture description: Closed Toroidal Ovoid, Sub-H(r))
monitor/baseline/02-wins.txt (WIN-053 listed as new category: 'Two-pole geomagnetic model')
monitor/baseline/01-home.txt (V51.0 homepage: 'Physical Model: Closed Toroidal Ovoid cavity')
No monitor.py available in repository — dome's code not directly accessible
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No monitoring infrastructure exists for WIN-053. The dome's monitor.py has 39 domains (matching V50.6's 39 WINs). WIN-053 was added in V51.0 (April 2026) and no new monitoring domain was created for the two-pole geomagnetic model, the B(r) equation, or the toroidal architecture. The homepage lists the 67/71 = 95.2% accuracy figure but no script computes this or validates the two-pole model against measured field data.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No monitoring exists. The B(r) equation and its claimed 20% RMS error are static claims on the website with no automated validation.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome's B(r) equation uses two fitted amplitudes (62,376 and 64,852 nT) that have no published derivation from dome parameters. These coefficients can be adjusted to match any observed dipole moment. The model also has the aetheric refractive index n(r) available as an escape hatch for any positional discrepancies.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: No significant discrepancies. Our review does not make specific claims about the dome's code for WIN-053, and no monitoring code exists for this WIN.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-053 was added in V51.0 (April 2026). The dome's monitor.py has 39 domains corresponding to the V50.6 WIN list. No new domain was added for the two-pole model, B(r) validation, or toroidal architecture. The polling data (monitor/changes/2026-04-05T23-08-40Z.json) confirms monitor_py_domains = 39 and no changes to monitoring infrastructure since V51.0 launched.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Earth's dipolar magnetic field has been described by standard physics since Gilbert (1600) and is modeled to high precision by IGRF-13 (168 spherical harmonic coefficients, degree 13) and CHAOS-7. The dipolar field arises from a liquid iron outer core dynamo. The dome model relabels this as 'aetheric toroidal circulation' — two exponential terms centered on the poles — but predicts nothing that the standard models don't already predict with far greater precision. The dome's B(r) equation is a two-parameter curve fit to the same field data that IGRF/CHAOS models describe using rigorous physical theory.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Earth's dipolar magnetic field has been measured since Gauss (1838). The IGRF has been published since 1900, with current-epoch models (IGRF-13, 2019) providing global field predictions. The dome's B(r) coefficients (62,376 and 64,852 nT) are evidently fitted to known field strengths at the magnetic poles (~58,500 nT north, ~66,000 nT south per current IGRF/WMM data). The dome did not predict these values before measurement — it adopted known measurements and fitted an exponential curve to them.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The exponential decay form e^(−r/8619) comes from the dome's λ_g constant, which is claimed to derive from dome geometry. However, the critical coefficients 62,376 and 64,852 nT have NO published derivation from dome parameters (disc_radius, firmament_height, or any dome geometric property). Section 3.5.8 of our review correctly notes these are fitted parameters. Furthermore, the dome provides no derivation of WHY a toroidal aetheric flow should produce an exponential field decay — in standard EM theory, a toroidal solenoid produces a 1/r field inside the torus, not an exponential. The equation form is chosen to fit data, not derived from geometry.

holes_found.description: GARBLED SENTENCE in detail_evidence. The text reads: '...contrast with CHAOS-7 and IGRF-13, which predict both poles from a single spherical harmonic expansion describing how two poles arise from its geometry, nor how they couple to produce observed field anomalies.' This sentence is grammatically broken — the 'describing how two poles arise from its geometry, nor how they couple' clause dangles. It appears to be a merge of two different sentences: one about CHAOS-7/IGRF and one about the dome's failure to describe its own geometry. A dome defender reading this would note the incoherent prose as evidence of sloppy analysis. The intended meaning seems to be: 'The dome model provides no unified equations describing how two poles arise from its geometry or how they couple — contrast with CHAOS-7 and IGRF-13, which predict both poles from a single spherical harmonic expansion.'
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite detail_evidence. Suggested: 'The dome model provides no unified equations describing how two poles arise from its toroidal geometry, how they couple, or why they should have similar strengths despite the 1,600:1 area asymmetry. Contrast this with CHAOS-7 and IGRF-13, which predict both poles — and the full multipolar structure — from a single spherical harmonic expansion of the geodynamo.'

holes_found.description: VERDICT CATEGORY MISMATCH. The primary argument — that flux conservation in the dome's own ring magnet geometry forbids equal-strength poles with a 1,600:1 area ratio — is a self-contradiction, not a data refutation. The logical structure is: 'Your toroidal geometry (ring magnet) + flux conservation → B_south ≈ B_north/1,600 ≈ 39 nT. But your fitted equation gives B_south ≈ 64,852 nT at r_S=0. Your geometry contradicts your equation.' This is the dome's own parameters contradicting each other — the hallmark of 'Self-Contradicted.' The external data (measured field strengths) supports the argument but the core logic doesn't depend on it. Under our verdict taxonomy, 'Self-Contradicted' means 'Dome's own geometry/equations contradict the claimed values,' which is exactly what's happening. 'Refuted by Data' means 'External measurements directly contradict the claim,' which is secondary here.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider changing verdict from 'Refuted by Data' to 'Self-Contradicted.' The flux conservation argument is a geometric self-contradiction: the dome's toroidal topology predicts wildly asymmetric pole strengths, but the dome's own B(r) equation gives similar-strength poles. If the verdict changes, the finding should be updated to: 'Dome\'s ring magnet geometry predicts B_south ≈ 39 nT via flux conservation; fitted equation gives 64,852 nT — a 1,660:1 discrepancy within the model.' NOTE: This would shift one WIN from 'Refuted by Data' (10→9) to 'Self-Contradicted' (11→12). Counts recompute automatically.

holes_found.description: V50.6 CONTRADICTION ARGUMENT IS UNFAIRLY FRAMED. The detail_evidence leads with 'the claimed two-pole system contradicts the same dome author's previous assertion (V50.6) that the field is a monopolar vortex. Switching between monopolar and dipolar descriptions indicates a lack of a fixed underlying mechanism.' In standard scientific practice, model evolution is expected and good — Newton → Einstein didn't invalidate physics. A dome defender would correctly argue: 'We improved our model based on new analysis. That's how science works. Your review penalizes us for doing exactly what you'd want us to do.' The argument as stated is a weak criticism that actually makes us look unreasonable. The REAL issue isn't that the model changed, but that: (1) the change is ad hoc — no new data or failed prediction motivated it, and (2) the new architecture contradicts its own geometry (flux conservation). We should lead with the stronger arguments.
holes_found.recommendation: Remove or substantially reframe the V50.6 contradiction argument. Instead of penalizing model evolution, note: (1) the architectural change from monopole to toroid was not motivated by any failed prediction or new observation — it appears to be a retroactive reinterpretation, and (2) the new toroidal architecture creates worse problems than it solves (flux conservation failure). If the V50.6 point is kept at all, frame it as: 'The transition from monopolar to toroidal was not accompanied by any new data, derivation, or acknowledgment of what the monopolar model got wrong — the change is asserted rather than derived.'

holes_found.description: The 1,600:1 area ratio is stated without derivation. Where does this number come from? A dome defender or curious reader cannot verify the claim. The calculation presumably is: A_pole (north pole aperture) ≈ some small area, A_sub (sub-terrestrial return path cross-section at the rim) ≈ large area. But what pole radius was used? The Axis Mundi isn't given a specific radius. If the dome's toroidal geometry means 'aether exits over the entire northern region and returns through the southern rim,' the area ratio depends entirely on where you draw the boundary — making the 1,600:1 figure somewhat arbitrary.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief derivation or explanation of the 1,600:1 area ratio. For example: if the North Pole source is treated as a circular aperture with radius ~500 km, A_pole ≈ 7.85 × 10⁵ km². The sub-terrestrial return at the Antarctic rim has area ≈ 2π × 20,015 × h_rim. If we use the total disc area π × 20,015² ≈ 1.26 × 10⁹ km², the ratio is ~1,600:1. Show the calculation so readers can verify.

holes_found.description: SUMMARY FINDING MISCHARACTERIZES ARGUMENT. The finding says 'Contradicts V50.6 monopolar vortex; no actual mechanism change.' But there IS an actual mechanism change — from monopolar to toroidal. The toroidal architecture with a sub-terrestrial return path IS a different mechanism. The finding should focus on why the mechanism change fails (flux conservation), not deny that it happened.
holes_found.recommendation: Update finding to something like: 'Toroidal geometry fails flux conservation: 1,600:1 area ratio forbids equal-strength poles; coefficients are fitted, not derived' or 'Ring magnet topology contradicts its own B(r) equation via flux conservation; coefficients are fitted parameters.'

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence mentions CHAOS-7 and IGRF-13 as contrast examples but doesn't explain to the reader what these models achieve that the dome's doesn't. Merely naming them without context weakens the comparison. A dome defender could say: 'Those are empirical curve fits too — just with more coefficients.'
holes_found.recommendation: Add brief context: IGRF-13 uses 168 spherical harmonic coefficients derived from physical theory (geodynamo) and validated against satellite measurements (Swarm), ground observatories, and repeat surveys. It predicts field strength, inclination, and declination at any point on Earth to ~50 nT accuracy. The dome's two-parameter exponential cannot reproduce the observed multipolar structure (e.g., the South Atlantic Anomaly's shape and position).

holes_found.description: The claim that the dome's B(r) 'drops global RMS from 61% to 20%' is reported without scrutiny. What does '61% RMS' mean — RMS of what, compared to what? Is 20% RMS actually good? IGRF-13 achieves ~50 nT RMS over the globe (~0.1% of typical field strength). If the dome's model has 20% RMS error, that's ~10,000–13,000 nT — enormously worse than IGRF. The 'improvement' from 61% to 20% should be contextualized as still terrible by scientific standards.
holes_found.recommendation: Add context: 'Even at 20% RMS, the model error is ~10,000 nT — two orders of magnitude worse than IGRF-13 (~50 nT). The improvement from 61% to 20% means the model went from catastrophically wrong to still very wrong.'

stronger_arguments.description: TOROIDAL SOLENOID PRODUCES 1/r FIELD, NOT EXPONENTIAL. In standard electromagnetic theory, the magnetic field inside a toroidal solenoid is B = μ₀NI/(2πr), where r is the distance from the axis of symmetry. This produces a 1/r dependence, not an exponential decay. If the dome's aetheric circulation is analogous to current flowing through a toroidal coil (which is how the architecture is described), then the field should decrease as 1/r from the central axis, not as e^(−r/λ). The dome's choice of exponential form is physically incompatible with the toroidal geometry it claims to be modeling. This is a much sharper self-contradiction than the flux conservation argument alone — it shows the equation form itself is wrong for the claimed geometry.
sources: Griffiths, Introduction to Electrodynamics, 4th ed., Section 5.3.3 (Toroidal solenoid)

stronger_arguments.description: THE COEFFICIENTS REVEAL THE MODEL IS A CURVE FIT, NOT A DERIVATION. The B(r) equation has coefficients 62,376 and 64,852 nT. If these came from dome geometry, they should be expressible in terms of dome parameters: disc_radius (20,015 km), firmament_height (9,086 km), λ_g (8,619 km), κ (1.67 nT/µGal). No such derivation exists on the dome site. Moreover, the coefficients are suspiciously close to observed magnetic pole field strengths: ~58,000-60,000 nT at the north magnetic pole and ~65,000-67,000 nT at the south magnetic pole (per WMM2025). The coefficients appear to be the observed pole field strengths padded to account for the exponential not being exactly 1 at r=0. This makes the entire 'prediction' circular: fit coefficients to observed data, then declare the match a 'confirmed WIN.'

THE B(r) EQUATION CANNOT REPRODUCE THE SOUTH ATLANTIC ANOMALY. The dome model's two-term exponential is radially symmetric around each pole. It cannot produce the observed South Atlantic Anomaly — a localized region of anomalously low field strength centered near Brazil (~20-25°S, 25-30°W). The SAA's shape, position, and evolution are driven by multipolar field structure (specifically the reverse flux patches in the outer core), which requires many spherical harmonic coefficients to describe. A two-parameter exponential produces smooth radial decay — no localized anomalies. Yet the dome model claims 14 WINs about SAA behavior (004, 005, 024, 035, 040, 041, etc.) using the same magnetic field that its B(r) equation cannot represent.
sources: Terra-Nova et al. (2019), EPSL 508, 117-125, DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2018.12.019 — South Atlantic Anomaly origin from reverse flux patches
Hartmann & Pacca (2009), PNAS 106(51), 22440-22444 — SAA time evolution and core dynamics

kernel_of_truth.description: The toroidal architecture is a genuine conceptual improvement over V50.6's monopolar vortex. A monopole cannot produce the observed dipolar field (two poles of similar strength). The dome author recognized this and introduced a return path to create a closed circuit — this is physically sensible reasoning for any magnetized body. The toroidal topology is also consistent with the general principle that magnetic field lines must form closed loops (∇·B = 0). The attempt to model both poles with a single scale length λ_g = 8,619 km shows an effort toward parameter parsimony, and the idea that a fundamental length scale might govern multiple geophysical phenomena is not inherently unreasonable (in standard physics, the Earth's radius of ~6,371 km does play this role).
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Good intuition doesn't substitute for quantitative physics. The toroidal geometry introduces a fatal constraint: flux conservation. In any closed magnetic circuit, Φ = B·A must be conserved. The dome's ring magnet has wildly asymmetric areas at the two 'poles' (small north pole aperture vs. vast sub-terrestrial return), so flux conservation requires wildly different field strengths — the opposite of what's observed and what the B(r) equation claims. The equation itself uses an exponential decay form that is physically incompatible with toroidal geometry (which produces 1/r decay per standard EM theory). The coefficients are fitted to observations rather than derived from geometry. And the model cannot reproduce any multipolar structure (SAA, secular variation, westward drift) that IGRF-13 handles routinely. The toroidal idea was sound in principle but the execution reveals it was never worked through quantitatively.

deeper_analysis:
WIN-053 has multiple issues that need addressing, but the core argument (flux conservation failure) is strong and should be preserved. The main problems are presentational rather than substantive.

First, the verdict category. The flux conservation argument is fundamentally about the dome's own geometry contradicting its own equation — the hallmark of 'Self-Contradicted.' The dome says 'our geometry is a toroid' and 'our equation gives similar pole strengths,' but flux conservation in a toroid with 1,600:1 area ratio requires the southern return field to be ~1,600× weaker. This is the dome's parameters fighting each other. It's similar to the Schumann self-contradiction (dome cavity predicts ~22 Hz, not 7.83 Hz) and the tidal pattern self-contradiction (local moon can't produce two bulges). I recommend changing the verdict to 'Self-Contradicted.'

Second, the V50.6 contradiction argument is the weakest part of our text and should be either removed or reframed. In any scientific context, model evolution is desirable. The dome defender's obvious response — 'We improved our model, that's how science works' — makes US look unreasonable for criticizing it. The real issue is that the 'improvement' was ad hoc (no failed prediction motivated it, no new data drove it) and creates worse problems than it solves.

Third, the detail_evidence has a garbled sentence that makes our review look sloppy. The clause about CHAOS-7/IGRF doesn't parse grammatically.

Fourth, we should strengthen the argument by adding the 1/r vs exponential point: standard EM theory says toroidal geometry produces a 1/r field dependence, not exponential. The dome's choice of exponential form is incompatible with the geometry it claims.

Fifth, we should note that the B(r) coefficients (62,376 and 64,852) are suspiciously close to observed pole field strengths, strongly suggesting they were fitted to data rather than derived from geometry. This makes the entire 'WIN' circular.

The relationship between WIN-053 and Section 3.5.8 is important. Section 3.5.8 already makes the single-pole collapse and fitted coefficient arguments in detail. WIN-053's detail text should either reference that section or avoid duplicating it. Currently they overlap but neither references the other.

Overall, WIN-053 is one of the more interesting WINs to review because the dome model actually attempted to improve its geomagnetic framework. The toroidal idea shows genuine physical reasoning. But the execution fails quantitatively on multiple fronts: flux conservation, field dependence form, and coefficient provenance. Our review should acknowledge the improvement attempt while clearly showing why it fails — this is the charitable but rigorous approach that builds credibility.

citations_verified: CHAOS-7: https://spacecenter.dk/files/magnetic-models/CHAOS-7/ — DTU Space, valid URL for the CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field model. This is the correct institutional page.
IGRF-13: https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/international-geomagnetic-reference-field — NOAA/NCEI, valid URL for the International Geomagnetic Reference Field. Correct institutional page.
citations_unchecked: The 1,600:1 area ratio figure — this is an internal calculation, not an external citation. Should have a derivation shown, currently stated without supporting math.

The 61% to 20% RMS improvement claimed by the dome — this appears in our detail_extra as a reported claim from the dome site. We did not verify whether the dome's site actually states these specific numbers or how they were computed.
